items.q: What does Maslow's hierarchy of needs propose about motivation?
items.correct: Humans are motivated by a hierarchy of needs (physiological, safety, social, esteem, self-actualisation) — lower needs must be substantially met before higher ones motivate.
items.misconception: Maslow said money is the most important motivator at work.
items.random_wrong: Maslow said employees must be promoted every year.
items.why: Students conflate Maslow with Taylor (who DID emphasise pay). Maslow's whole point is that once basic needs are met, higher needs (recognition, self-actualisation) motivate — not more money.

items.q: How does HRM differ from traditional personnel management?
items.correct: HRM treats people as a strategic asset and integrates human resource policies with business strategy; personnel management is more administrative and reactive, handling hiring, pay and disputes.
items.misconception: HRM and personnel are exactly the same thing — just a name change.
items.random_wrong: HRM only applies to large multinational companies.
items.why: The shift from personnel to HRM is conceptual — from administrative function to strategic partner. Students treat the relabel as cosmetic, missing the strategic management literature behind it.

items.q: What is Herzberg's distinction between hygiene factors and motivators?
items.correct: Hygiene factors (pay, conditions, policies) prevent dissatisfaction when present but don't motivate; motivators (recognition, achievement, responsibility) create positive satisfaction.
items.random_wrong: Hygiene factors are about workplace cleanliness.
items.why: Pay is a hygiene factor — its absence demotivates but its presence doesn't motivate beyond a point. Students treat pay as the prime motivator; Herzberg's theory directly challenges this.

items.q: In Maslow's hierarchy, the highest level is:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Self-actualisation. “Safety” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Herzberg classifies pay primarily as a:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Hygiene factor. “Motivator” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many levels in Maslow's hierarchy of needs?
items.why: Exact answer: 5. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
